Spherical mirrors, also known as curved mirrors, can have surfaces that curve inward or outward.
Activity 10.1: Exploring with a Spoon
  • Use a spoon to observe reflections.
  • Curved inward: inverted image.
  • Curved outward: smaller, erect image.
Concave Mirrors
A concave mirror curves inward and is part of a schematic representation, producing a unique reflective surface.
Convex Mirrors
Convex mirrors curve outward and have different reflective characteristics compared to concave mirrors.
Manufacturing Process
  • Spherical mirrors are created by grinding and polishing flat glass into a curved surface.
  • Metal coatings determine whether mirrors are concave or convex.
Reflecting and Non-Reflecting Surfaces
Schematic representations of mirrors depict the non-reflecting surface as shaded.
Activity 10.2: Identifying Mirror Types
  • Place mirrors on the table, reflecting surfaces up.
  • Observe from the side to determine inward or outward curvature.
Concept of Hollow Sphere
Spherical mirrors give the impression of being sections of an imaginary hollow sphere.
Reflective Coating in Mirrors
A thin layer of aluminium, when applied to the outer or inner surface, classifies mirrors as concave or convex.
Reflection and Perception
Curved mirrors alter perception by producing images that can be different in size and orientation compared to the real object.

sections.label: What Are the Characteristics of Images Formed by Spherical Mirrors?
sections.html:
Characteristics of Spherical Mirrors
Spherical mirrors include concave and convex mirrors. These mirrors create images with distinct characteristics based on their shape.
Experiment with Spherical Mirrors
  • Use concave and convex mirrors with a small toy or object.
  • Place mirrors upright; keep the object 3-4 cm in front.
Images in Spherical Mirrors
  • Images in concave mirrors are initially enlarged and erect.
  • In convex mirrors, images are always erect and diminished.
Dynamic Image Size
The distance between the object and mirror affects image size and orientation in both concave and convex mirrors.
Lateral Inversion Effect
Lateral inversion is observed in images formed by plane, concave, and convex mirrors alike.
Plane vs. Spherical Mirrors
  • Plane mirrors always create an erect image the same size as the object.
  • Spherical mirrors create images that vary in size and orientation.
Image Characteristics in Concave Mirrors
As the object moves away from the mirror, concave mirrors invert their image and vary in image size.
Image Characteristics in Convex Mirrors
Convex mirrors consistently produce smaller, erect images compared to the actual object.
Applications of Concave Mirrors
  • Used in torch reflectors, vehicle headlights, and dental mirrors for enlarged views.
Applications of Convex Mirrors
  • Used in vehicle side mirrors for a wider view and in stores for surveillance.
Telescope Mirrors
Most modern telescopes use large concave mirrors for enhanced distant object viewing.
Distinguishing Mirror Types
The type of image can indicate whether a mirror is plane, concave, or convex.
Visibility and Safety Use of Convex Mirrors
Convex mirrors installed at road junctions and sharp turns increase driving safety.
Physical Design of Convex Mirrors
A convex mirror is curved outward, offering a wider angle of reflection and coverage.
Concave Mirror Usage
Dentists use concave mirrors for precise dental inspection due to their enlarging properties.

sections.label: What Are the Laws of Reflection?
sections.html:
Reflection Basics
  • Reflection is when light bounces off surfaces.
  • The law of reflection states that the angle of incidence is equal to the angle of reflection.
Tools for Reflection Observation
  • Use items like a plane mirror, a torch, comb, paper clip, white paper, and black paper to observe light reflection.
Creating a Light Beam
  • Block comb openings with black paper to create a thin light slit.
  • Direct the slit light onto a mirror surface to observe beam reflection.
Angles in Reflection
  • The incident ray is the light ray approaching the mirror.
  • The reflected ray is the light bouncing off the mirror.
  • The angle made by the incident ray with the normal is the angle of incidence.
  • The angle made by the reflected ray with the normal is the angle of reflection.
Normal to the Plane Mirror
  • A normal line is drawn at a 90-degree angle at the point of incidence on the mirror.
Experiment with Angles
  • Measure angles of incidence and reflection, notice they are equal.
  • Test different angles and observe changes in reflected light direction.
Plane of Incidence
  • Incident ray, normal at the point of incidence, and the reflected ray lie in the same plane.
Concept of Multiple Rays
  • When multiple parallel rays hit a plane mirror, they reflect in parallel as well.
Reflection in Different Mirrors
  • Concave mirrors converge reflected rays.
  • Convex mirrors diverge reflected rays.
Curved Surface Phenomenon
  • Rays reflecting off curved mirror surfaces result in different convergent or divergent patterns.
Converging Light on Paper
  • A concave mirror focuses sunlight onto a small area, producing heat that can ignite paper.
Solar Concentration
  • Solar concentrators use mirrors to intensify sunlight for heating purposes, including electricity generation.
Real-life Application Activity
  • Use a concave mirror and sunlight to create a hotspot on paper.
  • This concentrated light can produce sufficient heat.
Precautionary Measures
  • Ensure safety by avoiding direct eye or face exposure to intense mirror-reflected light.
Practical Uses of Solar Energy
  • Solar furnaces use concentrated sunlight for melting metals, similar to electric furnaces.

sections.label: What Is a Lens?
sections.html:
Understanding Lenses
A lens is a piece of transparent material with curved surfaces, typically made of glass or plastic.
Lenses and Refraction
Unlike mirrors, lenses allow light to pass through them, affecting how we see objects through a lens.
Types of Lenses
  • A lens thicker at the middle compared to the edges is called a convex lens.
  • A lens that is thinner at the middle compared to the edges is called a concave lens.
Convex Lens Characteristics
Convex lenses converge light rays, making them also known as converging lenses.
Concave Lens Characteristics
Concave lenses diverge light rays, earning them the name diverging lenses.
Activity with Convex Lens
When an object is placed behind a convex lens at a small distance, it appears erect and larger.
Increasing Distance with Convex Lens
As the object's distance from the convex lens increases, it first appears inverted and then diminishes in size.
Activity with Concave Lens
Objects always appear erect and reduced in size when seen through a concave lens.
Convex Lens Observations
  • Initial image: Enlarged and erect.
  • Increased distance: Inverted and smaller.
Concave Lens Observations
  • Image remains: Erect and diminished in size regardless of distance.
Experimenting with Light Beams
A thin glass plate lets light beams pass through unchanged, whereas lenses alter them.
Light through Convex Lens
Convex lenses converge (focus) parallel beams of light, drawing them to a point.
Light through Concave Lens
Concave lenses make parallel beams of light diverge, spreading them outward.
Investigating Lenses
  • Use a comb, torch, and lenses to observe light beam behavior.
  • Convex lenses focus light, concave lenses spread it.
Drawing Findings
Visual representations show light convergence through convex and light spread through concave lenses.

sections.label: How Does the Moon's Appearance Change and Why?
sections.html:
The International Kite Festival - Meera attended the International Kite Festival in Ahmedabad on Makar Sankranti. - During the festival, she noticed the Moon was visible in the daytime.
The Moon's Visibility - Meera was surprised to see the Moon during the day, having thought it only appeared at night. - The Moon looked different from a full circle, initiating her curiosity.
Nature of the Moon's Light - The Moon is spherical and shines by reflecting sunlight. - The Moon is not fully visible every night because only part of it is illuminated.
The Changing Shape of the Moon - The Moon goes through phases due to its position relative to the Earth and Sun. - These phases determine how much of the illuminated part is visible from Earth.
Observation of the Moon - An activity was designed to document the Moon's appearance over a month. - Observations included sighting time, size of bright portions, and separation from the Sun.
The Phases of the Moon - The waning period starts after a full Moon and lasts about two weeks. - The waxing period is when the visible portion grows, lasting another two weeks.
Moon's Movement and Visibility - The Moon's position changes daily relative to the Sun, affecting visibility. - The Moon rises about 50 minutes later each day, affecting appearances in the sky.
Understanding Moon's Light - Moon doesn't emit its light; it reflects sunlight. - We observe different portions of the illuminated Moon each day.
Simple Moon Observation Experiment - Using a ball and a lamp, one can simulate the Moon's phases. - Holding the ball at different positions relative to light demonstrates phase changes.
The Mechanism Behind Moon Phases - The phases result from the Moon's changing position as it revolves around Earth. - When the whole illuminated portion faces the Earth, a full Moon is visible.
Observational Analysis - The study of apparent changes helps understand the Earth's rotation and Moon's revolution. - Observations explain why different regions on Earth see similar Moon phases.
Common Misconceptions - The Moon's phases aren't due to Earth's shadow; that results in a lunar eclipse. - Earth, Moon, and Sun's orientation lead to visible phase changes.
Eclipse Occurrences - Lunar eclipses occur during full Moons, and solar eclipses during new Moons. - They are not regular monthly events due to Moon's orbit tilt.
Reflecting on Lunar Observations - Activities and observations challenge misconceptions and deepen understanding. - Exploring lunar concepts clarifies celestial mechanics.
A Broader Perspective - Analyzing Moon phases highlights the complexity of celestial dynamics. - Structured activities foster curiosity and learning about the night sky.

sections.label: How Did Calendars Come into Existence?
sections.html:
The Nature of a Day
The Sun's apparent motion is mainly due to Earth's rotation, creating the day cycle.
A mean solar day lasts 24 hours, indicating the Sun's highest point in the sky from one day to the next.
Observing a Day's Duration
- Use a stick and observe shadow lengths.
- Record the shortest shadow time to find the mean solar day duration.
Lunar Phase Cycles
The cycle of the Moon’s phases is about 29.5 days and serves as the basis for a one-month time measurement.
The Cycle of Seasons
- Earth’s revolution around the Sun leads to a near 365 and a quarter days cycle.
- This cycle defines a solar year and is crucial for season changes.
Lunar Calendars Explained
12 lunar months fit into one cycle of seasons, making up a lunar year of around 354 days.
Effects of Lunar Calendars
The lunar year doesn't align with seasons due to its 11-day shorter duration than the solar year.
Solar Calendars Overview
Solar calendars, like the Gregorian calendar, synchronize time with seasons by accounting for the 365 days and a quarter cycle.
Leap Year Adjustments
Leap years, with an extra day added every four years, help align solar calendars with Earth's actual year cycle duration.
Sidereal and Tropical Year
- The sidereal year measures the Earth's orbit duration relative to fixed stars.
- The tropical year is observed between successive spring equinoxes.
Our Calendric Heritage
Ancient astronomers determined the length of the year by keen sky observations despite limited tools and knowledge.
Sun Path Observations
Sun's rising and setting points vary with seasons, linked to Uttarayan and Dakshinayan movements.
Luni-solar Calendars
- These calendars adjust to synchronize lunar months with a solar year by adding an intercalary month.
- They are common in many parts of India.
Luni-solar Month Naming
Names like Chaitra, Vaisakha, and Ashwin come from Indian luni-solar traditions with Amant and Purnimant versions.
The Indian National Calendar
Matches the solar year, beginning on March 22, with months from traditional Indian names and leap year adjustments.
Meghnad Saha's Contribution
Saha, an astrophysicist, played a vital role in calendar reforms, leading efforts to unify different Indian calendars.

sections.label: Are Festivals Related to Astronomical Phenomena?
sections.html:
Festivals based on Lunar Calendars
  • Many Indian festivals follow the phases of the Moon.
  • Examples include Diwali, Holi, and Buddha Purnima.
Festivals based on Luni-Solar Calendars
  • Dates generally shift, but typically less than a month.
  • An intercalary month is added every few years.
Festival Date Shifts
  • Festivals aligned to lunar phases can vary each year in the Gregorian calendar.
  • Eid-ul-Fitr is influenced by the lunar calendar.
Solar Sidereal Calendar Festivals
  • Includes Makar Sankranti, Pongal, and Bihu.
  • These festivals happen almost consistently on specific dates annually.
Influence of Solstice and Equinox
  • Originally connected to solstices or equinoxes.
  • Dates shift forward due to Earth's axial wobble.
Makar Sankranti Date Shift
  • Occurs slightly later in the Gregorian calendar every 71 years.
Influence of Sunrise on Festival Dates
  • Lunar phase festivals base on sunrise timing.
  • Results in potential date differences across Indian regions.
Centralized Calendar System
  • Rashtriya Panchang published for uniformity in festival dates.
  • Uses celestial calculations by the Government of India.
Impact on Music and Dance
  • Ragas like Chandrakauns and Chandranandan inspired by the Moon.
  • Bharatanatyam and other dances use Moon imagery.
Visual Arts Influence
  • Madhubani and Warli paintings feature the Moon and Sun.
  • Common in tribal sculptures and pottery.
Lunar Interpretations in Music
  • Ragas exhibit moon imagery in melodic expressions.
Hand Gesture Symbolism
  • Chandrakala and Ardhachandran gestures in Bharatanatyam relate to the Moon.
Wider Artistic Context
  • Moon's imagery influences Kathak, Odissi, and Kuchipudi too.
Tribal Influence on Art
  • Gond and Saura tribes depict the Moon in art.
Significance in Daily Life
  • Moon and Sun depictions reflect their importance in daily Indian life.

sections.label: Why Do We Launch Artificial Satellites in Space?
sections.html:
Artificial Satellites
Artificial satellites are man-made objects launched into orbit around Earth to perform various functions such as communication, navigation, and scientific research.
Orbit and Duration
Satellites orbit around 800 km above Earth's surface and typically take about 100 minutes to complete one orbit.
Uses of Satellites
Satellites contribute to communication, navigation, weather monitoring, disaster management, and scientific research.
ISRO's Contributions
The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) is renowned for launching satellites that assist in various activities, from mapping to space exploration.
Cartosat Series
The Cartosat satellites support high-quality imaging to assist in mapping, urban planning, and disaster management.
Bhuvan Platform
Bhuvan utilizes satellite imagery to provide detailed information on terrain, soil, land use, and vegetation in India.
AstroSat
AstroSat is an ISRO mission that observes stars and celestial objects, enhancing scientific research in space.
India's Space Missions
India’s notable missions include Chandrayaan (lunar exploration), Aditya L1 (solar study), and Mangalyaan (Mars mission).
Education Initiatives
ISRO collaborates with students to develop and launch small satellites like AzaadiSat, InspireSat-1, and Jugnu, fostering scientific curiosity.
Spotting Satellites
  • Observe the sky before sunrise or after sunset for moving points of light.
  • Use apps or websites to identify passing satellites.
Space Junk
A significant concern is space debris from defunct satellites and rocket parts, which pose a collision risk to functional satellites.
Addressing Space Debris
Countries are working together on strategies to remove or mitigate space debris hazards.
Vikram Sarabhai
Father of the Indian Space Program, Vikram Sarabhai significantly contributed to the development of India's space research initiatives.
Vikram Sarabhai Space Centre
Located in Thiruvananthapuram, the VSSC is crucial in developing rockets and launch vehicle technology.
Artificial Satellite Detection
  • Look for moving lights in a clear sky.
  • Satellites move quickly and can be seen with the naked eye.

sections.label: How Do We Experience and Interpret Our Surroundings?
sections.html:
Elephants In India Elephants frequently enter farms and villages in states like Odisha, Jharkhand, West Bengal, Assam, and Chhattisgarh.
Reasons for Elephants Entering Farms Elephants seek food such as bananas and sugarcane when vegetation is scarce and waterholes dry up in their natural habitat.
Impact of Human Activities Deforestation for infrastructure development like roads and buildings contributes to habitat shrinkage and forest drying.
Adaptation Challenges Sudden environmental changes affect the survival capacity of animals like elephants that are adapted to forest life.
Wildlife Corridors Ecologists have marked corridors to facilitate safe wildlife movement, connecting forest habitats and preventing human-wildlife conflict.
Interconnectedness of Nature Nature's elements show a complex interconnectedness, highlighting the importance of studying environmental components.
Diversity in Habitats Habitats can vary from ponds to forests to agricultural farms, each with unique living and non-living components.
Biotic and Abiotic Components * Biotic: Living organisms like plants and animals * Abiotic: Non-living elements like air, water, and soil
Habitat Specific Conditions Conditions in habitats determine the types of organisms that can survive there, like land organisms versus aquatic organisms.
Resources In Pond Habitat Ponds provide oxygen, food, shelter, and space to grow for organisms like fish, frogs, snails, and plants.
Species in Pond Habitat Includes frogs, turtles, snakes, dragonflies, mosquitos, snails, ducks, and plants like algae, diatoms, duckweeds, and lotus.
Coexisting Organisms Organisms in the same habitat use resources differently to thrive, exemplifying harmony and coexistence in nature.
Activity 12.1 - Habitat Exploration * Explore habitats such as a pond or forest. * Identify both living and non-living components.
Activity Observations Both habitats will have biotic and abiotic elements, with variations in the specific types of living beings.
The Role of Environmental Study Understanding habitats and their components fosters greater awareness and conservation efforts.

sections.label: Who All Live Together in Nature?
sections.html:
Understanding Populations
Organisms of the same species living in a habitat make up a population.
Observing Fish in Pond
Observing a group of fish in a pond depicts a population of that fish species.
Activity 12.2 Setup
Students divide into groups to study populations by counting plants or animals in a set area.
Conducting Activity 12.2
Mark a square meter in a garden, identify four organisms, count, and record their numbers.
Recording Observations
Compile data of observed organisms' numbers in a table.
Population Definition
Population refers to organisms of the same type living in one area at a time.
Community Description
A community consists of different populations of organisms sharing a single habitat.
Interactions in a Community
Interactions among different species in a community help them survive.
Components of a Community
Plants, animals, and microorganisms form the biotic components of a community.
Understanding Pollination
Pollination involves moving pollen from stamens to carpels, aiding fruit and seed formation.
Reproductive Parts of a Flower
A flower consists of stamens (male) and carpels (female) as reproductive components.
Transfer Agents for Pollination
Pollen is transported by wind, water, insects, bats, and birds during pollination.
Flower Structure
Flowers have sepals, petals, stamens, and carpels as their primary structures.
Importance of Pollination
Pollination is crucial for developing fruits and seeds, ensuring plant reproduction.
Resource Competition
If a habitat houses only one organism type, competition for resources could arise.

sections.label: Does Every Organism in a Community Matter?
sections.html:
Role of Organisms in a Community Understanding the significance of each organism in an ecosystem by studying their interactions and impacts.
Fish Influence on Seed Production A study compares seed production in plants around Pond A with fish and Pond B without fish.
Fish and Flowering Plants Pond A, with fish, had many flowering plants, while Pond B, without fish, had fewer.
Dragonfly Population Comparison Analyze the dragonfly population around Pond A (less dragonflies) versus Pond B.
Fish Predation on Dragonfly Larvae Fish eat dragonfly larvae, reducing dragonfly numbers in Pond A.
Dragonfly Diet Impact Dragonflies, which eat flies, bees, and butterflies, have their numbers reduced, affecting pollinator availability.
Pollinators and Seed Production With fewer dragonflies, more pollinators (bees, flies, butterflies) thrive, aiding seed production.
Ecosystem Interactions Biotic and abiotic interactions include fish, dragonflies, pollinators, plants, temperature, and nutrients.
Overfishing Impact Exploring how human activities like overfishing can alter the balance within an ecosystem.
Balance of Biotic Components Fish, dragonflies, and pollinators contribute to the ecosystem's dynamic balance.
Abiotic Influence Temperature, water, and nutrients shape the environment's physical conditions.
Biotic and Abiotic Interactions Understanding the integrated relationship between living organisms and their non-living environment.
Fish and Plant Symbiosis Fish indirectly support plant reproduction by controlling dragonfly populations.
Human Influence on Ecosystems Human intervention, such as overfishing, can disrupt the established ecological equilibrium.
Conservation Importance Recognizing the importance of protecting each organism to sustain community diversity and functionality.

sections.label: What Are the Different Types of Interactions Among Organisms and their Surroundi
sections.html:
Essentials for Organisms
Plants and animals need air, water, soil, and sunlight to grow and survive.
Dependence on Abiotic Components
Biotic components depend on abiotic things for nutrition, respiration, and reproduction.
Types of Interactions in Ecosystems
  1. Between biotic components
  2. Between biotic and abiotic components
  3. Between abiotic components
Activity Identification
Learn and identify interactions in Fig. 12.5 between biotic and abiotic components.
Criterion 1 Analysis
Determine the effect of interactions between abiotic and biotic components on life processes.
Criterion 2 Exploration
Understand how interaction between two abiotic components influences habitat characteristics.
Criterion 3 Observation
Assess how biotic interactions affect resource availability for life processes.
Recording Observations
Record observations of biotic and abiotic interactions in Table 12.3 for detailed analysis.
Ecosystem Formation
Biotic and abiotic components interact to form ecosystems, which can be large or small.
Types of Ecosystems in Nature
  • Aquatic Ecosystems: Ponds, rivers, lakes
  • Terrestrial Ecosystems: Forests, farmlands, trees
Dependency in Ecosystems
Organisms depend on non-living components like sunlight, soil, air, and water for survival.
Importance of Photosynthesis
Plants make their food through photosynthesis, releasing oxygen, retaining soil moisture, and more.
Activity 12.5 Classification
Classify organisms based on feeding habits using Table 12.4 and identify producers and consumers.
Feeding Habits Categorization
  • Herbivores: Eat only plants
  • Carnivores: Eat only animals
  • Omnivores: Eat both plants and animals
Role of Organisms in Food Production
Autotrophs like plants make their own food, while heterotrophs depend on others for nourishment.

sections.label: Who Eats Whom?
sections.html:
Feeding Relationship in Ecosystems
Organisms in a given ecosystem interact through feeding relationships, determining who eats whom.
Grassland Ecosystem Example
A grassland ecosystem features organisms like grass, frogs, hares, foxes, grasshoppers, snakes, and eagles.
Interdependent Food Chains
The relationship of food consumption involves each organism eating the preceding one in a linear sequence.
Example Food Chain
Grass → Grasshopper → Frog → Snake → Eagle
This describes a simple food chain showing interconnected feeding relationships.
Trophic Levels Explained
  • The **Producers** like green plants occupy the first trophic level.
  • **Herbivores** like hares reside at the second level.
  • **Small carnivores** like frogs are at the third level.
  • **Large carnivores** such as eagles are at the higher trophic level.
Activity 12.7 Description
Fig. 12.10a illustrates a crop field with millets, mice, and eagles, each of which is counted to demonstrate population data.
Creating a Trophic Pyramid
The organisms recorded in Fig. 12.10a are organized in a pyramid structure, indicating trophic hierarchy.
Food Web Exploration
Food chains within an ecosystem interconnect to create a wider network called a food web.
Understanding Food Webs
Multiple organisms may consume the same prey, forming a complex web of food relationships.
Visualizing Ecosystem Interactions
Figures like 12.10b help visualize the pyramid structure and relationships between different trophic levels.
Pyramid of Numbers in Ecosystems
In a trophic pyramid, populations typically decrease at higher trophic levels due to lesser available energy.
Activity Observation and Action
Examining Fig. 12.11 involves tracing missing feeding relationships and adding the appropriate arrows.
Significance of Ecosystem Waste
During its life cycle, each organism produces waste, contributing to the ecosystem's nutrient recycling.
Linking and Trace Activity Results
In Fig. 12.11, note how complex an ecosystem's feeding network becomes when organisms are interconnected.
Ecosystem Feeding Dynamics
Analyzing different figures helps to understand how organisms feed and relate within an interconnected ecosystem.

sections.label: What Happens to Waste in Nature?
sections.html:
Fungi and Decomposition
During the rainy season, mushrooms often appear on dead plants or trees. These fungi grow on dead matter, breaking it down into simpler substances.
Role of Microorganisms
Microorganisms like fungi and bacteria decompose complex substances in dead plants and animals, returning crucial nutrients to the soil.
Soil Nutrients
The nutrients in soil predominantly originate from decomposition, which makes it fertile for plant growth.
Decomposers Explained
Decomposers, also called saprotrophs, consume rotten matter. "Sapro" means rotten and "trophs" means food.
Nature's Recycling
In nature, every element is reused. Nothing goes to waste, as it's all part of the ecosystem's natural cycle.
Insect Role in Decomposition
Insects like beetles and flies assist in breaking down animal waste, aiding the recycling of nutrients back into the environment.
Migratory Birds in India
India hosts numerous migratory birds seeking refuge from harsh climates while searching for food.
Importance of Migratory Birds
Migratory birds enrich habitats while serving as pollinators and seed dispersers, linking different ecosystems.
Migratory Birds and Pest Control
As predators of insect pests, migratory birds help farmers by controlling pest populations, leading to healthier crops.
Demoiselle Crane Migration
During the winter, Demoiselle Cranes migrate to Khichan village, demonstrating a significant seasonal movement.
Identify Local Migratory Birds
Identify which birds appear in your locality during winter and understand their migration patterns and purposes.
Collecting Information
Gather postal stamps showcasing migratory birds from the Indian Postal Department, learning about their journeys.
Showcasing Stamps
Display your migratory bird stamp collection in a school library to raise awareness about these species.
Promoting Bird Awareness
Use your compilation of postal stamps to educate peers on migratory birds' roles and the habitats they visit.
Environment Preservation through Awareness
Understanding the symbiotic roles within ecosystems helps in promoting conservation efforts.

sections.label: How Does One Change Lead to Another?
sections.html:
One Small Change Effects Changes in the environment often begin with one small event, like the dying of plants due to pollution.
Cascade of Effects When plants die, less oxygen is produced, affecting the fish population in the pond.
Diminished Fish Population The reduction in fish numbers can lead to a decrease in the pond's consumers.
Insect Overpopulation Fewer fish lead to more insects settling in nearby areas.
Farmers' Predicament An increase in insects in farmland compels farmers to use pesticides.
Environmental Repercussions The use of pesticides could harm the environment and lead to more issues, such as soil quality degradation.
Case Study of Frog Leg Export India exported frog legs, particularly of Hoplobatrachus tigerinus, leading to ecological imbalance.
Consequence of Reduced Frog Population The drop in frog numbers led to an increase in agricultural pests.
Rise in Pesticide Use Increased pests resulted in more pesticide use, affecting soil and water quality.
Environment and Health Impact Excessive pesticide use negatively impacts the environment and human health.
Regulatory Action The Indian government halted frog leg exports to counteract environmental harm.
Balance in Ecosystems Ecosystems remain stable when organisms and environmental interactions maintain resources.
Dynamic Nature of Ecosystems Ecosystem balance is not fixed but can change with human or natural disruptions.
Concept of Stable Populations Stability arises through balance among organisms and resources.
Adversity of Human Activity Human interference, such as pollution and large-scale harvesting, often disrupts ecological balance.

sections.label: How Do Interactions Maintain Balance in Ecosystems?
sections.html:
Interactions Maintaining Ecosystem Balance Feeding relationships control population size within ecosystems.
Competition for Resources Organisms compete for resources like food, water, space, and sunlight to maintain balance.
Population Control Competition prevents overpopulation, ensuring ecosystem stability.
Mutualism Relationship Both organisms benefit, such as honeybees pollinating flowers.
Commensalism Relationship One organism benefits, the other is unaffected, like orchids on trees.
Parasitism Relationship One organism benefits by harming another, exemplified by ticks on dogs.
The Web of Life Ecosystems are made of interconnected interactions forming a web of life.
Understanding Forest Ecosystems A.J.T Johnsingh explored forest life through animal behaviors.
Wildlife Tracking Johnsingh pioneered wildlife studies using modern tracking technologies.
Predator-Prey Dynamics Predators like tigers rely on prey for survival, highlighting interconnected species.
Healthy Prey Populations Johnsingh demonstrated that sustaining prey is vital for predator survival.
Youth Inspiration Johnsingh inspired young minds to pursue wildlife studies and conservation.
Wildlife Protection in India Conservation urged to protect forests and biodiversity in India.
Balance of Nature Graphs Figures 12.15 and 12.16 illustrate organism interactions.
Role of Scientists Scientists like Johnsingh further our understanding of ecological dynamics.

sections.label: What Are the Benefits of an Ecosystem?
sections.html:
Biotic and Abiotic Interdependence Biotic and abiotic components of an ecosystem depend on each other for survival. Fosters life processes essential for various species.
Benefits of Ecosystems * Fresh air, fertile soil, food, fibers, timber, and medicines. * Water and food from aquatic ecosystems. * Aesthetic and recreational value. Encourages human well-being through nature's interconnectedness.
The Sundarbans: A Vital Ecosystem The Sundarbans house the largest global mangrove forest. Act as a buffer against storms and floods by slowing winds and waves.
Threats to the Sundarbans * Mangrove cutting for fuelwood and farming. * Illegal hunting and overuse of forest resources. Pollution from industrial waste disrupts ecosystems.
Ecosystem Threats in India Deforestation, overuse of resources, invasive species, and pollution threaten. Damage extends to forests, rivers, wetlands, and coastal areas.
Protection Through Reserved Areas Examples: Jim Corbett National Park, Manas National Park. Play significant roles in conserving wildlife and their habitats for future generations.
Human-made Ecosystems Fish ponds, farms, and parks designed for human needs. Require regular care and management compared to natural ecosystems.
Fast Facts on Farming and Ecosystems Farming becomes unsustainable with overuse of chemicals and water. Building sustainable farming practices requires understanding ecosystems.
Water and Soil Management Heavy irrigation affects soil organisms like earthworms and snails. Monoculture reduces crop diversity and impacts pollinator populations.
Surveying Farming Practices Prepare a list of questions about pesticide use and recycling. Interact with farmers to gather data for a report.
Impact of Synthetic Fertilizers Offers increased crop production but reduces soil microorganisms. Leads to decreased fertility and increased soil erosion over time.
Alternatives to Conventional Farming Organic and natural farming methods utilize minimal synthetic inputs. Promote sustainable practices to maintain soil and environmental health.
Vrikshayurveda and Soil Health Traditional texts emphasize soil nourishment using organic manure. Promote composted materials like Kunapa Jala for enhancing fertility.
Conserving Ecosystems Protecting forests involves correct land and resource management. Community participation plays a key role in ecosystem sustainability.
Actions for Future Ecosystem Protection Engage communities in protecting vital ecosystems around them. Adopt practices that balance conservation with human needs.

revision.chapter: Our Home: Earth, a Unique Life Sustaining Planet

sections.label: Why Is Earth a Unique Planet?
sections.html:
Earth's Unique Quality
All known forms of life only exist on Earth within a delicate layer on its surface.
Home to Diverse Life
Earth's surface includes mountains, rivers, forests, animals, and humans.
Comparative Thickness
Earth's crust is compared to the skin of an apple.
Layer of Life
Only a very thin layer of Earth's surface hosts all life.
Mountain Heights
From the tallest mountain peak to the deepest ocean trench, life exists in Earth's crust.
Supportive Environment
The thin crust supports and thrives with life forms.
Special Nature of Earth
The delicate layer of Earth's crust supports life, making it unique in the universe.
Crust Description
The crust is the outermost solid layer of Earth and incredibly thin.
Planetary Comparison
Even with billions of planets, Earth's conditions are conducive to life.
Delicate Balance
Life thrives in a balanced manner on the thin layer where all life persists.
Life Sustaining Feature
The thin crust of Earth is what sustains life and its diversity.
Size Analogy
  • If Earth is the size of an apple, the crust's thickness is comparable to the apple’s skin.
Diversity on Crust
  • The crust contains diverse geographical features like forests and bodies of water.
Life's Habitat
Life exists and thrives only on the thin crust of Earth’s vast planet.
Unique Among Billions
Among billions of planets, Earth is special in hosting life, attributed to its crust.
Earth as a Unique Planet
There are billions of planets in the universe, but Earth is the only one known to support life in all its forms.
Components of Earth's Surface
Earth's surface includes mountains, rivers, forests, animals, and people.
The Thin Layer of Life
Life exists on a very thin layer on Earth's surface, which is the crust.
Mountain Heights and Ocean Depths
The crust includes both the tallest mountains and the deepest ocean trenches.
Proportion of Earth's Crust
  • If Earth were the size of an apple, its crust would merely be as thin as the apple's skin.
Earth's Delicate Layer
This thin crust is the delicate, life-supporting layer that constitutes Earth's unique characteristic.
Mountain Peaks
Mountain peaks rise from the crust, forming some of the tallest structures on Earth.
Subterranean Depths
Subterranean trenches stretch deep below sea level, showcasing the vast extent of Earth's crust.
Comparison of Crust's Proportion
  • The crust is tiny in comparison to the whole size of Earth.
Importance of Earth's Crust
Earth's thin crust layer plays a crucial role in supporting all known forms of life.
Earth as an Apple
Using a common analogy, the Earth's crust's thinness is likened to an apple's skin.
Life Thrives Here
Despite its thinness, the crust supports diverse life forms that thrive on its surface.
Earth's Special Nature
The unique nature of Earth lies in this delicate layer that supports life.
Life's Dependence on the Crust
All known life forms rely on this thin layer of Earth's surface for sustenance.
The Delicate Layer
This thin crust is what makes Earth stand out among billions of other planets.

sections.label: What Do the Planets of Our Solar System Look Like?
sections.html:
Energy from the Sun
All planets in the solar system derive energy from the Sun. This affects their temperatures based on proximity.
Heat on Venus
Venus is hotter than Mercury despite being farther from the Sun due to its dense atmosphere trapping heat.
Role of the Greenhouse Effect
The greenhouse effect traps heat on Venus and Earth through atmospheric gases like carbon dioxide.
Difference in Greenhouses
The atmospheric greenhouse effect is different from plant greenhouses, which retain air heat inside enclosed glass walls.
Earth's Optimal Position
Earth's location in the "Goldilocks Zone" ensures a suitable climate for liquid water, crucial for life.
Why Earth is the Blue Planet
Most of Earth's surface is covered in water, giving its "Blue Planet" appearance when viewed from space.
Mars Exploration
Mars, nearing the Sun's habitable zone, has past conditions hinting at water, intriguing scientists about potential life.
Earth's Circular Orbit
The nearly circular orbit of Earth keeps climatic conditions moderate, preventing extreme seasonal changes.
Atmospheric Balance on Earth
Earth's size and gravity retain an atmosphere, facilitating life and providing protection against solar radiation.
Function of Earth's Atmosphere
The Earth's atmosphere contains oxygen for life and an ozone layer protecting against harmful ultraviolet rays.
India's Mars Mission
India's Mangalyaan mission in 2013 demonstrated cost-effective space exploration, studying Martian atmosphere and surface.
The Earth's Magnetic Field
Generated by molten iron movements, Earth's magnetic field shields the planet from harmful cosmic and solar particles.
The Role of Cosmic and Solar Particles
Earth's magnetic field repels harmful particles from the Sun and cosmic rays, essential for life protection.
Impact of Earth's Features
Earth's position, size, atmosphere, and magnetic field collectively facilitate the emergence and sustenance of life.
Scientific Curiosity
Exploring Mars exemplifies science's evolving quest for knowledge and understanding of life beyond Earth.

sections.label: What Makes the Earth Suitable for Life to  Exist ?
sections.html:
Earth's Unique Conditions for Life
Earth possesses the essential conditions for life, facilitated by the harmonious interaction between living and non-living entities.
Key Natural Resources
Resources crucial for sustaining life include air, water, sunlight, soil, and minerals.
Life Processes in Plants and Animals
Plants and animals engage in vital life processes that contribute to Earth's ecosystem dynamics.
Air, Water, and Sunlight
Oxygen in the atmosphere is used for respiration by living organisms.
  • Sunlight allows plants to perform photosynthesis.
  • Photosynthesis releases oxygen essential for life.
The Greenhouse Effect
Sun's radiation warms the Earth.
  • The atmosphere traps some heat.
  • Keeps Earth's temperature suitable for water in liquid form.
Water's Role
About 70% of Earth's surface is water, forming the hydrosphere.
  • It's key in transporting nutrients in plants and vital for animal health.
Earth's Hydrosphere Biodiversity
Variety of life, from plankton to whales, thrives in aquatic environments like oceans and rivers.
Weather and Rainfall Cycle
Water vapor forms clouds, impacting rainfall crucial for agriculture and ecosystems.
Soil, Rocks, and Minerals
Earth's crust is rich in nutrients necessary for plant growth and contains vital minerals.
Significance of the Geosphere
Composed of soil, rocks, and minerals forming a foundation for living organisms.
The Biosphere
Consists of all life forms and their habitats in land, water, and air.
Food Chain Dynamics
  • Plants synthesize food.
  • Animals rely on plants and other animals.
  • Decomposers recycle nutrients back to the soil.
The Importance of Ecological Balance
The collaborative interaction of land, water, air, and organisms ensures Earth's habitability.
Impact of Human Actions
Human activities like deforestation can affect ecological balance, impacting soil, air, and living organisms.
Preserving Earth's Balance
Protecting natural resources is crucial for sustaining life on Earth and maintaining ecological harmony.
Earth's Support System
Earth has the right conditions to support life, with interconnections between living and non-living elements being crucial for sustaining it.
Key Natural Resources
Key resources like air, water, sunlight, soil, and minerals play significant roles in life on Earth.
Air, Water, and Sunlight
Oxygen in the atmosphere is vital for respiration, while carbon dioxide and water are essential for photosynthesis in plants.
Photosynthesis
In the presence of sunlight, plants convert carbon dioxide and water into food, releasing oxygen needed for respiration.
Greenhouse Effect
The greenhouse effect traps some heat, keeping Earth warm enough for liquid water and preventing it from becoming too cold.
Hydrosphere
Comprising 70% of Earth's surface, the hydrosphere includes ponds, lakes, rivers, and oceans, housing diverse life forms.
Water's Role
Water transports nutrients, aids digestion, and regulates temperature in organisms, ensuring health and survival.
Aquatic Life
Oceans and freshwater bodies provide critical habitats for countless species, from plankton to whales.
Atmospheric Moisture
Water vapor in air forms clouds, bringing precipitation that refills natural water sources and shapes ecosystems.
Geosphere
The Earth's crust consists of rocks, soil, and minerals, forming a foundation for ecosystems and human life.
Soil Nutrients
Soil contains essential nutrients like nitrogen and potassium, derived from rock breakdown and organic matter decomposition.
Geodiversity
The variety in landforms, rocks, and soils contributes to unique habitats and ecosystems.
Biosphere
The biosphere includes all living organisms and their habitats on land, in water, and in the air.
Life's Interconnections
Plants, animals, and decomposers interact within ecosystems, promoting nutrient cycling and energy flow.
Balance Importance
Life on Earth relies on the balance between natural elements, supporting ecosystem health and planetary habitability.

sections.label: What Keeps Life from Disappearing?
sections.html:
Reproduction Importance
Life would cease to exist without reproduction, as it is essential for the continuity and persistence of all organisms.
Genetic Transmission
Animals reproduce young that resemble them due to the genetic instructions, stored as genes, passed from parents.
Genes: Nature's Blueprints
Genes are detailed instruction manuals in cells, guiding the formation of blood, bones, and other vital functions.
Inheritance and Variability
Reproduction ensures species survival, while also introducing variations for adaptation and evolution.
Adaptive Evolution
Over generations, small genetic changes can lead to significant evolutionary developments, as observed in camels and bacteria.
The Puzzle of Reproduction
The dual nature of reproduction results in young ones resembling their parents while also showcasing variations.
Asexual Reproduction
No genetic mixing occurs, leading offspring to be identical copies of the single parent.
Sexual Reproduction and Mixing
Involves two parents, resulting in offspring with a mix of traits from both.
Vegetative Propagation
Plants can reproduce asexually by part of the plant, such as a stem or leaf, growing into a new individual.
Role of Gametes
Special reproductive cells carrying half of genetic material from parents are responsible for creating a new life form.
Genetic Uniqueness in Offspring
Offspring differ from parents and siblings due to the unique mix of genetic information in gametes.
Flower Reproduction
In plants, pollen (male gamete) combines with ovules (female gamete) leading to seed and fruit formation through fertilization.
Seed Dispersal
Fruits aid in spreading seeds far from the parent plant when consumed by animals.
Animal Reproductive Cells
In animals, male and female gametes are known as sperm and eggs, respectively.
Embryonic Development in Birds and Mammals
  • In birds, embryo development takes place outside the mother's body.
  • In mammals, development occurs inside the female's body, ensuring nutritional supply until birth.

sections.label: What Are the Threats to Life on Earth?
sections.html:
Delicate Balance of Earth Life on Earth relies on a balance between living and non-living elements interacting in harmony.
Impact of Human Actions Human activities disrupt this balance, threatening the natural equilibrium necessary for sustaining life.
Triple Planetary Crisis Today's biggest environmental challenges are climate change, biodiversity loss, and pollution.
Burning Fossil Fuels The combustion of coal and oil releases greenhouse gases which trap heat and contribute to global warming.
Carbon Cycle Disruption Excessive carbon dioxide from fossil fuels exceeds the Earth's absorption capacity, leading to heat buildup.
Consequences of Climate Change Increased temperatures can melt ice caps, raise sea levels, and trigger extreme weather events.
Impact on Ecosystems Destroying natural habitats leads to the loss of species, disrupting the food chain and ecosystem balance.
Air Pollution Effects Factory emissions and vehicle fumes can harm respiratory health and contribute to smog and acid rain.
Global Efforts for Preservation International agreements like the Montreal Protocol and Paris Agreement aim to protect the environment.
Water and Soil Pollution Industrial and agricultural waste contaminates water and soil, threatening ecosystems and food safety.
Interconnection of Earth Systems The hydrosphere, biosphere, atmosphere, and geosphere are interconnected, so damage to one affects the others.
Renewable Energy Using solar and wind energy helps reduce greenhouse gases and protect the climate.
Sustainable Practices Local communities can conserve resources by reusing, repairing, and recycling to minimize pollution.
Personal Responsibility Individuals can make a difference through small actions like saving energy and spreading awareness.
Collaborative Action Sustaining life on Earth requires cooperation from both local inhabitants and global leaders to ensure a safe future.

ncert.chapter: The Invisible Living World: Beyond Our Naked Eye

sections.label: Probe and Ponder
sections.html:
Q 1

What might you see if the invisible world around you became visible?

Solution
  • Imagine if we could see the tiny, invisible world of microorganisms with our naked eyes! You’d see a bustling, colorful world full of life all around you-on your hands, in the air, in water, and even in your food! Here’s what you might notice:
  • Shapes and Movements: Tiny creatures like bacteria (rod-shaped, spherical, or spiral), amoebas (shapeless blobs that wiggle), and fungi (thread-like or mushroom-like) would be everywhere. Some might be swimming, crawling, or just floating.
  • Crowded Places: A drop of pond water would look like a busy city with millions of microorganisms, like paramecium (slipper-shaped) zooming around, eating tiny bits of food.
  • On Your Skin: You’d see bacteria living harmlessly on your skin, looking like tiny dots or chains, helping protect you or just hanging out.
  • In Food: In curd or bread, you’d see yeast (tiny ovals) making bubbles of gas or bacteria turning milk into tangy curd.
  • Colors and Glow: Some microorganisms, like certain algae, might glow or look green, red, or even shiny under light.
  • It would feel like discovering a secret world, like a jungle or an ocean, but too small to see without a microscope!
Q 2

How might your observation of this hidden world change the way you think about size, complexity, or even what counts as ‘living’?

Solution

Seeing the invisible world would make you think differently about size, complexity, and life itself. Here’s how:

  • Size: You’d realize that even the tiniest things, like bacteria (smaller than a grain of sand), can do big jobs, like breaking down waste, making food spoil, or helping plants grow. Size doesn’t decide importance!
  • Complexity: Microorganisms may look simple (like a single cell), but they’re super complex. For example, bacteria can “talk” to each other using chemicals, move toward food, or even fight diseases. A single cell can be as busy as a whole factory!
  • What Counts as Living: You might wonder if a single-celled amoeba, moving and eating, is as “alive” as a human or plant. Microorganisms grow, reproduce, and respond to their surroundings, so they’re definitely living, even if they’re tiny and don’t have brains or hearts. This might make you rethink life- maybe even a virus (which needs a host to multiply) could seem partly alive!
Q 3

How do these tiny living beings interact with each other?

Solution

Microorganisms don’t live alone- they interact with each other like neighbors in a community! Here’s how they might “talk” or work together in their hidden world:

  • Helping Each Other: Some bacteria and fungi team up. For example, in soil, fungi provide nutrients to bacteria, and bacteria help fungi break down food. In your stomach, good bacteria work together to digest food and keep you healthy.
  • Competing for Space and Food: Microorganisms fight for space and nutrients. In a drop of water, bacteria might race to eat sugar before algae do, or they might release chemicals to push others away.
  • Living Together: Some microorganisms form groups called biofilms (like a slimy layer on rocks or teeth). In biofilms, bacteria stick together, share food, and protect each other from harm.
  • Predator and Prey: Bigger microorganisms, like amoebas, eat smaller ones, like bacteria, by swallowing them. It’s like a tiny food chain in the microscopic world!
  • Chemical Communication: Bacteria use chemicals to “talk” to each other (called quorum sensing). For example, they might signal, “There’s enough of us- let’s make a biofilm!” or “Attack that harmful bacteria!”
Q 4

Share your questions

Solution

Seeing the invisible world and learning about microorganisms sparks so many questions! Here are some questions you might have, and you can add your own:

  • What do microorganisms eat to stay alive?
  • Can microorganisms see or feel each other, or do they just bump into things?
  • Why are some bacteria helpful (like in curd) and others harmful (like causing diseases)?
  • How do microorganisms survive in tough places, like hot springs or salty water?
  • Could we use microorganisms to clean up pollution or make new medicines?
  • Do microorganisms ever “sleep” or rest, or are they always active?
  • What would happen if all microorganisms disappeared from Earth?

sections.label: Keep the curiosity alive
sections.html:
Q 1

Various parts of a cell are given below. Write them in the appropriate places in the following diagram: Nucleus, Cytoplasm, Chloroplast, Cell wall, Cell membrane, Nucleoid.

Solution

Common to Plant, Animal and Bacterial Cells:

  • Cytoplasm: Fills the cell and holds organelles in both plant and animal cells.
  • Cell membrane: Controls what enters and exits in both plant and animal cells.
  • Nucleus: The control center with DNA, found in both plant and animal cells.

Only in Plant Cells:

  • Chloroplast: Found only in plant cells, helps in photosynthesis to make food.
  • Cell wall: Found only in plant cells, gives strength and support.

Only in Animal Cells:

  • None of the given parts are exclusive to animal cells. (Animal cells may have other parts like centrioles, but they’re not in the list.)

Only in Bacterial Cells:

  • Nucleoid: Found only in bacterial cells, it’s a region where DNA is stored, not inside a proper nucleus.
  • Cell wall: Bacteria have a cell wall, but it’s different from a plant’s cell wall in composition.
Q 2

Aanandi took two test tubes and marked them A and B. She put two spoonfuls of sugar solution in each of the test tubes. In test tube B, she added a spoonful of yeast. Then she attached two incompletely inflated balloons to the mouth of each test tube. She kept the set-up in a warm place, away from sunlight.

(i) What do you predict will happen after 3-4 days? She observed that the balloon attached to test tube B was inflated. What can be a possible explanation for this?

  1. Water evaporated in test tube B and filled the balloon with water vapour.
  2. The warm atmosphere expanded the air inside test tube B, which inflated the balloon.
  3. Yeast produced a gas inside test tube B which inflated the balloon.
  4. Sugar reacted with warm air, which produced gas, eventually inflating the balloon.


(ii) She took another test tube, 1/4 filled with lime water. She removed the balloon from test tube B in such a manner that the gas inside the balloon did not escape. She attached the balloon to the test tube with lime water and shook it well. What do you think she wants to find out?

Solution
  1. (i) Prediction and Explanation:
    After 3-4 days, the balloon on test tube B will inflate, but the balloon on test tube A will not.
    The correct explanation is:
    Yeast produced a gas inside test tube B which inflated the balloon.
    Yeast, a type of fungus, breaks down sugar in warm conditions, producing carbon dioxide gas. This gas fills the balloon, making it inflate. Test tube A has no yeast, so no gas is produced, and the balloon stays the same.
  2. Purpose of Lime Water Test:
    Aanandi wants to find out if the gas in the balloon from test tube B is carbon dioxide. When she shakes the gas with lime water, if the lime water turns milky, it confirms the gas is carbon dioxide, as lime water reacts with carbon dioxide to form a cloudy substance. This shows yeast produced carbon dioxide in test tube B.
Q 3

A farmer was planting wheat crops in his field. He added nitrogen-rich fertiliser to the soil to get a good yield of crops. In the neighbouring field, another farmer was growing bean crops, but she preferred not to add nitrogen fertiliser to get healthy crops. Can you think of the reasons?

Solution
  • The farmer growing beans didn’t add nitrogen fertiliser because bean plants have special bacteria called Rhizobium in their root nodules. 
  • These bacteria take nitrogen from the air and turn it into nutrients the bean plants can use, making the soil naturally rich in nitrogen. 
  • Wheat crops don’t have these bacteria, so the other farmer needed fertiliser to add nitrogen for good growth. 
  • By growing beans, the farmer saves money and keeps the soil healthy without chemicals.
Q 4

Snehal dug two pits, A and B, in her garden. In pit A, she put fruit and vegetable peels and mixed it with dried leaves. In pit B, she dumped the same kind of waste without mixing it with dried leaves. She covered both the pits with soil and observed after 3 weeks. What is she trying to test?

Solution
  • Snehal is testing how dried leaves affect the breakdown of fruit and vegetable peels into manure. 
  • In pit A, mixing peels with dried leaves helps microorganisms (like bacteria and fungi) decompose the waste faster, turning it into nutrient-rich manure. 
  • In pit B, without dried leaves, decomposition is slower because microorganisms work less effectively. She wants to see if adding dried leaves makes better manure in less time.
Q 5

Identify the following microorganisms:

  1. I live in every kind of environment, and inside your gut.
  2. I make bread and cakes soft and fluffy.
  3. I live in the roots of pulse crops and provide nutrients for their growth.
Solution

Based on the chapter, the microorganisms are:

  1. Bacteria: They live in all environments, like water, soil, air, and inside our gut, helping with digestion (as mentioned in Section 2.4).
  2. (Yeast: This fungus makes bread and cakes soft and fluffy by producing carbon dioxide gas during fermentation (as in Activity 2.8).
  3. Rhizobium: This bacterium lives in root nodules of pulse crops (like beans) and traps nitrogen from the air to make nutrients for plants 
Q 6

Devise an experiment to test that microorganisms need optimal temperature, air, and moisture for their growth.

Solution

Experiment to Test Microorganism Growth:

  • Materials: 3 small bowls, bread pieces, water, yeast powder, a warm place, a refrigerator, a sealed plastic bag.
  • Steps:
    1. Take three bowls (A, B, C) and place a small piece of bread in each.
    2. In bowl A, add a pinch of yeast and a few drops of water, then cover it and keep it in a warm place (like near a window).
    3. In bowl B, add a pinch of yeast and water, then put it in a refrigerator (cold, moist, with air).
    4. In bowl C, add a pinch of yeast, but don’t add water, and seal it in a plastic bag (warm, dry, no air).
    5. Observe all bowls after 2–3 days.
  • Expected Results:
    • Bowl A (warm, moist, with air) will show the most growth, like mould or fluffy yeast, because microorganisms grow best in warm, moist conditions with air.
    • Bowl B (cold) will show little or no growth, as cold slows microorganisms (like in Activity 2.9 with cold milk).
    • Bowl C (dry, no air) will show no growth, as microorganisms need moisture and air.
  • Conclusion: Microorganisms need optimal temperature (warm), moisture, and air to grow well.
Q 7

Take 2 slices of bread. Place one slice on a plate near the sink. Place the other slice in the refrigerator. Compare after three days. Note your observations. Give reasons for your observations.

Solution

Observations:

  • The bread slice near the sink will likely have mould (green or white patches) after three days.
  • The bread slice in the refrigerator will have little or no mould and look mostly unchanged.

Reasons:

  • Near the sink, the bread is in a warm, moist place with air, perfect for microorganisms like fungi to grow and form mould (like in Section 2.4, where microbes grow on food).
  • In the refrigerator, the cold temperature slows down microorganism growth, preventing mould (like cold milk not curdling in Activity 2.9). Microorganisms need warmth and moisture to grow, which the refrigerator lacks.
Q 8

A student observes that when curd is left out for a day, it becomes more sour. What can be two possible explanations for this observation?

Solution

Two reasons why curd becomes more sour when left out for a day:

  1. Bacteria Keep Working: Curd has bacteria like Lactobacillus that feed on milk sugar (lactose) and produce lactic acid, making curd sour. When left out in warm conditions, these bacteria multiply and produce more lactic acid, increasing sourness.
  2. Warm Temperature Helps Bacteria: Warm conditions speed up bacterial activity, causing them to ferment the curd faster and produce more lactic acid. This makes the curd taste more sour.
Q 9

Observe the set-up given in figure and answer the following questions.

  1. What happens to the sugar solution in flask A?
  2. What do you observe in test tube B after four hours? Why do you think this happened?
  3. What would happen if yeast was not added in flask A?
Solution
  1. The sugar solution in flask A, mixed with yeast, ferments. Yeast breaks down the sugar, producing carbon dioxide gas and a small amount of alcohol, making the solution bubbly.
  2. In test tube B, the lime water turns milky after four hours. This happens because the carbon dioxide gas from flask A (produced by yeast) reacts with lime water, forming a cloudy substance. This confirms yeast produced carbon dioxide (similar to the balloon test in Question 2).
  3. If yeast was not added to flask A, no carbon dioxide gas would be produced because yeast is needed to ferment the sugar. The sugar solution would stay unchanged, and the lime water in test tube B would not turn milky.

sections.label: Discover, design, and debate
sections.html:
Q 1

India has a long history of biogas production. One of our oldest biogas plant was set up in late 1850s. Find out about the Biogas Program initiated by the Ministry of New and Renewable Energy, Government of India. 

Solution

Discover: India has been using biogas for a long time, starting in the late 1850s! Biogas is a clean fuel made from organic waste like cow dung, kitchen scraps, and plant material. The Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE) in India runs the National Biogas and Manure Management Programme (NBMMP) to help people set up biogas plants in rural and semi-urban areas. This program started to provide clean energy for cooking, lighting, and even small power needs, while also reducing pollution and helping farmers.Key Points about the Biogas Program:

  • Purpose: To make clean fuel from organic waste, improve sanitation, reduce greenhouse gases, empower women by reducing cooking smoke, and create jobs in villages.
  • How It Works: Biogas is produced through a process called anaerobic digestion, where microorganisms break down organic waste (like cow dung) without oxygen, producing a gas (55-65% methane, 35-44% carbon dioxide) and nutrient-rich slurry (bio-manure).
  • Types of Biogas Plants: Small plants (1-25 m3/day) for households and medium plants (25-2500 m3/day) for power generation (3-250 kW) or heating/cooling.
  • Benefits: Provides clean cooking fuel (like LPG), reduces indoor air pollution, saves money on LPG refills, and produces bio-manure to replace chemical fertilizers.
  • Support: The government gives subsidies (financial help) to families and farmers to set up biogas plants. For example, extra support is given in Northeast India, hilly areas, and for SC/ST communities.
  • Achievements: By 2014, about 47.5 lakh biogas plants were installed, with a target of 1.1 lakh more in 2014-15. The program continues until 2025-26 with a budget of ₹858 crore for biogas and other bioenergy projects.
  • Design: Imagine you’re designing a small biogas plant for a village home. It needs 50-60 m2 of space, a regular supply of cow dung, and water to mix into a slurry. Draw a simple model showing a digester tank, gas outlet, and slurry storage. Think about how to make it easy to use for a family!

Debate: Discuss with friends: “Should every village in India have a biogas plant?” Consider benefits (clean fuel, less pollution) versus challenges (cost, maintenance, availability of waste).

Q 2

Fermented food items like fermented soybeans and fermented bamboo shoots are consumed as traditional food in some parts of India. With the help of your parents and teachers, list some traditional food items from your area that utilise the process of fermentation. Investigate the ingredients used in the preparation of these fermented food items; the method of preparing them; the microorganism responsible for the fermentation of the food, and the cultural and nutritional importance of the fermented food.

Solution

Discover: Fermentation is a process where microorganisms like yeast or bacteria break down sugars and starches in food to produce acids, alcohol, or gases. This makes food tasty, easier to digest, and longer-lasting. In India, many traditional foods are fermented, especially in different regions. Let’s explore some examples from various parts of India, as you can ask your parents or teachers about local foods in your area.

Examples of Traditional Fermented Foods:

Dahi (Curd): Common across India.

  • Ingredients: Milk, starter culture (a bit of old curd with live bacteria).
  • Preparation: Boil milk, cool it slightly, add a spoonful of curd, and let it sit in a warm place for 6-8 hours until it thickens.
  • Microorganisms: Lactic acid bacteria (e.g., Lactobacillus species) turn milk sugar (lactose) into lactic acid, giving curd its tangy taste.
  • Cultural Importance: Used in daily meals, religious rituals, and festivals (e.g., curd rice in South India). It’s cooling and loved in summers.
  • Nutritional Importance: Rich in probiotics (good bacteria) that help digestion, boost immunity, and provide calcium and protein.

Idli/Dosa (South India):

  • Ingredients: Rice, urad dal (black gram), water, salt.
  • Preparation: Soak rice and dal separately, grind into a batter, mix, and let it ferment for 8-12 hours in a warm place until bubbly. Cook as idlis (steamed) or dosas (pancakes).
  • Microorganisms: Lactic acid bacteria (Lactobacillus) and yeast (Saccharomyces cerevisiae) ferment the batter, producing carbon dioxide for fluffiness.
  • Cultural Importance: A staple breakfast in South India, served with chutney and sambar, symbolizing simple, healthy eating.
  • Nutritional Importance: High in carbohydrates, protein, and probiotics; easy to digest and low in fat.

Gundruk (Northeast India, e.g., Sikkim, Darjeeling):

  • Ingredients: Leafy greens (mustard, radish, or cauliflower leaves).
  • Preparation: Leaves are wilted, crushed, packed tightly in a container, and fermented for 7-10 days. Then dried and stored.
  • Microorganisms: Lactic acid bacteria (Lactobacillus plantarum) ferment the leaves, producing lactic acid.
  • Cultural Importance: A traditional dish in Northeast India, often eaten as a side dish or in soups, reflecting local farming culture.
  • Nutritional Importance: Rich in vitamins, minerals, and probiotics; preserves greens for year-round use.

Kinema (Fermented Soybeans, Northeast India):

  • Ingredients: Soybeans.
  • Preparation: Soak soybeans, boil, wrap in leaves, and ferment for 1-3 days in a warm place until sticky.
  • Microorganisms: Bacillus subtilis ferments the beans, giving a unique smell and texture.
  • Cultural Importance: A protein-rich dish in Sikkim and Northeast, eaten with rice, part of tribal food traditions.
  • Nutritional Importance: High in protein, vitamins, and probiotics; a meat substitute for vegetarians.

Activity: Ask your parents or teachers about fermented foods in your area (e.g., dhokla in Gujarat, bamboo shoot pickle in Northeast, or handia in Odisha). List at least two local foods, their ingredients, and how they’re made.

Design: Create a poster showing how one fermented food (e.g., idli) is prepared, labeling the ingredients and steps. Include a fun fact about its cultural importance.

Debate: Discuss with classmates: “Are fermented foods healthier than non-fermented foods?” Talk about taste, nutrition, and shelf life.

Q 3

Study the different parts of a macro fungus mushroom using a magnifying glass and microscope/foldscope. Take the help of students from senior classes and explore the internal structure of different parts of mushrooms under the microscope/foldscope in your school laboratory.

Solution

Discover: Mushrooms are macro fungi, meaning they’re large enough to see without a microscope. They’re not plants but belong to the fungi kingdom. Common edible mushrooms (e.g., button or oyster mushrooms) have parts you can study with a magnifying glass and a microscope or foldscope (a low-cost paper microscope). Ask senior students or teachers to help in your school lab.

Parts of a Mushroom (using a magnifying glass):

  • Cap (Pileus): The top, umbrella-like part, often round or flat, protects the gills. Colors vary (white, brown, etc.).
  • Gills (Lamellae): Thin, blade-like structures under the cap, where spores (tiny seeds) are produced. Look like lines or folds.
  • Stalk (Stipe): The stem that holds up the cap, giving support. It may have a ring or skirt.
  • Ring (Annulus): A skirt-like structure on the stalk, left from the veil that covers young mushrooms (not all mushrooms have it).
  • Base (Volva): The bottom of the stalk, sometimes bulb-like or cup-shaped (seen in some mushrooms like Amanita).

Internal Structure (using a microscope/foldscope):

  • Spores: Tiny reproductive cells in the gills. Under a microscope, they look like small ovals or circles (4-6 µm in size for some mushrooms). Their color and shape help identify the mushroom.
  • Hyphae: Thread-like structures forming the mushroom’s body (mycelium). Under a microscope, they look like thin, branching tubes.
  • Basidia: Club-shaped cells in the gills where spores are formed. They’re visible under a microscope as small, elongated structures.

How to Study:

  1. Get a fresh mushroom (e.g., button mushroom from a market).
  2. Use a magnifying glass to observe the cap, gills, stalk, and ring clearly.
  3. In the lab, with help from seniors or teachers:
    • Cut a thin slice of the cap or gills using a blade (be careful!).
    • Place the slice on a slide, add a drop of water, and cover with a coverslip.
    • Observe under a microscope or foldscope at low (40x) and high (100x) magnification to see spores, hyphae, or basidia.
  4. Draw what you see and label the parts.

Design: Sketch a mushroom and label its parts (cap, gills, stalk, ring). Create a small chart showing what spores and hyphae look like under a microscope.
Debate: Discuss: “Should we eat all mushrooms we find in the wild?” Talk about why some mushrooms are edible and others are poisonous, using your observations of their structure.
Note: Always handle mushrooms with care, as some are toxic. Use only store-bought or lab-provided mushrooms for study.

Q 4

Interact with an entrepreneur and learn the steps for cultivation of mushroom.

Solution

Discover: Mushroom cultivation is a great way to grow healthy food using less land, water, and energy. It’s a popular business in India, especially for oyster or button mushrooms. By talking to a mushroom entrepreneur (or researching with your teacher’s help), you can learn the steps to grow mushrooms and start a small business.

Steps for Mushroom Cultivation (e.g., Oyster Mushrooms):

  1. Choose the Mushroom Type: Select an edible mushroom like oyster (Pleurotus species) or button (Agaricus bisporus), which are easy to grow and popular.
  2. Prepare the Spawn: Spawn is like the “seed” of mushrooms (mycelium grown on grains). Buy good-quality spawn from a trusted supplier or lab.
  3. Select a Substrate: Use agricultural waste like straw, sawdust, or corn stalks as the growing medium. Soak straw in water and boil or sterilize it to kill unwanted germs.
  4. Mix Spawn with Substrate: In a clean room, mix the spawn with the sterilized substrate and pack it into polythene bags with small holes for air.
  5. Incubation: Keep the bags in a dark, warm place (25-30°C) for 15-20 days. The mycelium (white, thread-like growth) spreads in the substrate.
  6. Fruiting: Move the bags to a cool, humid place (20-25°C, 80-90% humidity) with some light. Spray water daily to keep it moist. Mushrooms start growing in 5-10 days.
  7. Harvest: When mushroom caps are fully open, cut them at the base. You can harvest 2-3 times from one bag.
  8. Sell or Use: Sell fresh mushrooms in markets or dry them for longer storage. The leftover substrate (spent mushroom substrate) can be used as compost or for biogas production.

Tips from Entrepreneurs:

  • Keep everything clean to avoid contamination by other fungi or bacteria.
  • Maintain proper temperature and humidity (use a thermometer and water sprays).
  • Start small (e.g., 10-20 bags) to learn, then scale up for business.
  • Market mushrooms to local shops, restaurants, or online platforms.

Cultural and Economic Importance: Mushroom farming is eco-friendly, uses waste materials, and provides jobs. Mushrooms are nutritious (rich in protein, vitamins) and a meat substitute for vegetarians.

Design: Create a flowchart showing the steps of mushroom cultivation, from spawn to harvest. Add pictures or drawings to make it colorful.

Debate: Discuss: “Can mushroom farming be a good business for young people in villages?” Talk about costs, profits, and environmental benefits versus challenges like learning the process or finding buyers.

sections.label: Probe and Ponder
sections.html:
Q 1

How does your body respond to an infection such as common cold?

Solution

When you get an infection like the common cold, your body’s immune system fights back. You may get a runny nose, cough, fever, or feel tired- these are signs that your body is:

  • Trying to get rid of the germs (by sneezing, coughing, or producing mucus)
  • Raising your body temperature (fever) to make it harder for the germs to survive.
  • Making you rest so your body can recover.
Q 2

We rarely see cases of smallpox or polio these days, but diseases like diabetes and heart problems are more common. Why?

Solution
  • Smallpox and polio have become rare because of widespread vaccination, which protects people and stops these diseases from spreading.
  • Diseases like diabetes and heart problems are now more common because of modern lifestyle changes: people eat more processed/junk food, exercise less, and live longer lives. These are called non-communicable diseases and are often linked to habits rather than infections.
Q 3

Could climate change lead to new types of diseases?

Solution

Yes. Climate change can cause new types of diseases or make old ones more common. For example:

  • Warmer temperatures can help disease-carrying insects like mosquitoes (that spread dengue or malaria) survive in new places.
  • Changing weather can create more floods or droughts, which can contaminate water and lead to more infections.
  • Plants, animals, and microbes may behave differently, introducing new health risks to people.
Q 4

How do emotions like stress or worry affect us and make us sick?

Solution

Emotions such as stress, worry, or sadness affect not just your mind but your body too:

  • Long-term stress weakens the immune system, making it easier for you to fall sick.
  • It can cause headaches, stomach problems, trouble sleeping, or lead to unhealthy habits (like bad eating or skipping exercise).
  • Staying positive and managing stress helps keep both your body and mind healthy.
Q 5

Why do some groups of people get affected more than others during disease outbreaks?

Solution

Some people are more affected during disease outbreaks because of:

  • Weaker immune systems (young children, elderly, or those already sick)
  • Poor living conditions or hygiene, making it easier for germs to spread
  • Lack of access to vaccines, medicines, or clean water
  • Close contact with more people or limited knowledge about how to protect themselves.

So, health depends on your body, your habits, and your environment!

sections.label: Keep the curiosity alive
sections.html:
Q 1

Group the diseases shown in the images as communicable or non-communicable.

Solution

Communicable Diseases

  • Cold and flu: Spread from person to person (through air, droplets, etc.).
  • Typhoid: Spread through contaminated food and water.
  • Chickenpox: Spread from person to person (through air or direct contact).

Non-Communicable Diseases

  • Diabetes: Not caused by pathogens and does not spread from person to person; usually linked to lifestyle or genetic factors.
  • Asthma: Not infectious; related to environmental, genetic, or lifestyle causes.
Q 2

Diseases can be broadly grouped into communicable and non-communicable diseases. From the options given below, identify the non-communicable diseases. 

  1. Typhoid 
  2. Asthma 
  3. Diabetes 
  4. Measles
Q 3

There is a flu outbreak in your school. Several classmates are absent, while some are still coming to school coughing and sneezing.
What immediate actions should the school take to prevent further spread?

Solution
  • Advise sick students to stay home until they are fully recovered.
  • Inform parents and staff about the outbreak, its symptoms, and basic prevention steps.
  • Encourage frequent hand washing and provide soap or hand sanitizers in classrooms and washrooms.
  • Ensure classrooms are well-ventilated-open windows and doors if possible.
  • Clean and disinfect frequently touched surfaces like desks, doorknobs, and washroom taps regularly.
  • Remind everyone to cover their mouth and nose when coughing or sneezing (using a tissue, handkerchief, or elbow).
  • Discourage sharing of personal items such as water bottles, food, and handkerchiefs.
Q 4

There is a flu outbreak in your school. Several classmates are absent, while some are still coming to school coughing and sneezing.
If your classmate, who shares the bench with you, starts showing symptoms of the flu, how can you respond in a considerate way without being rude or hurtful?

Solution
  • Speak kindly and gently: You can say something like, "You don’t look well. Maybe you should tell the teacher or visit the school nurse."
  • Offer them a tissue or hand sanitizer if they are coughing or sneezing.
  • Encourage them to cover their mouth and nose while coughing or sneezing, if they aren’t already doing so.
  • Keep a reasonable distance if possible, but without making them feel excluded or embarrassed.
  • Do not tease or blame them - show understanding, as anyone can get sick.
Q 5

There is a flu outbreak in your school. Several classmates are absent, while some are still coming to school coughing and sneezing.
How can you protect yourself and others from getting infected in this situation?

Solution
  • Wash your hands often with soap and water, especially after touching shared surfaces or before eating.
  • Avoid touching your face (nose, mouth, eyes) after touching surfaces in class.
  • Use a mask if advised by the school - especially in crowded indoor spaces.
  • Maintain a safe distance from those who are coughing or sneezing.
  • Bring and use your own water bottle and stationery.
  • Eat nutritious food and get enough sleep to keep your immune system strong.
  • Tell your teacher if you start to feel unwell so that you can go home and recover, helping prevent spreading the illness.
Q 6

Your family is planning to travel to another city where malaria is prevalent. What precautions should you take before, during, and after the trip?

Solution

Before the trip:

  • Learn about malaria in the city you’re visiting.
  • Visit a doctor for advice - sometimes preventive medicines (antimalarial tablets) may be recommended.
  • Pack mosquito repellents, bed nets, and long-sleeved clothes.
